title: La Passion Vaincue by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
On the Banks of the Severn a desperate Maid
(Whom some Shepherd, neglecting his Vows, had betray'd,)
Stood resolving to banish all Sense of the Pain,

title: The Man And His Horse by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
Within a Meadow, on the way,
A sordid Churl resolv'd to stay,
And give his Horse a Bite;
Purloining so his Neighbours Hay,
That at the Inn he might no

title: Life's Progress by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
How gayly is at first begun
Our Life's uncertain Race!
Whilst yet that sprightly Morning Sun,
With which we just set out to run
Enlightens all the Pla

title: On the Death of the Honourable Mr. James Thynne by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
Farewell, lov'd Youth! since 'twas the Will of Heaven
So soon to take, what had so late been giv'n;
And thus our Expectations to destroy,

title: To A Husband by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Love Poetry Lines
content:
This is to the crown and blessing of my life,
The much loved husband of a happy wife;
To him whose constant passion found the art
To win a stubborn an

title: On The Hurricane by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
You have obey'd, you WINDS, that must fulfill
The Great Disposer's righteous Will;
Throughout the Land, unlimited you flew,
Nor sought, as heretofore,

title: Hope by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Deep Poetry Lines
content:
The Tree of Knowledge we in Eden prov'd;
The Tree of Life was thence to Heav'n remov'd:
Hope is the growth of Earth, the only Plant,
Which either Heav

title: The Dog And His Master by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Deep Poetry Lines
content:
NO better Dog e'er kept his Master's Door
Than honest Snarl, who spar'd nor Rich nor Poor;
But gave the Alarm, when any one drew nigh,
Nor let pretend

title: The Tree by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Deep Poetry Lines
content:
Fair tree! for thy delightful shade
'Tis just that some return be made;
Sure some return is due from me
To thy cool shadows, and to thee.
When thou to

title: Glass by Anne Kingsmill Finch — Deep Poetry Lines
content:
O Man! what Inspiration was thy Guide,
Who taught thee Light and Air thus to divide;
To let in all the useful Beams of Day,
